text: BAMBUHI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, rising in the Sierra Alegre e Marcella, and flowing into the San Francisco, on the l. bank, after a NE course of about 80 m.

BAMBUHY (Anna do), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, 80 m. WSW of Pitangui. BAMEENY, BOMINY, or BANINY, an island in the bay of Bengal, to the E of the embouchure of the Megna. It is about 12 m. in length and 5 m. in average breadth, and is very little raised above the level of the tide, from the action of which, in conjunction with that of the mighty streams which unite in the Megna, it appears to have derived its existence. It has a salt manufactory in connection with the government agencies of Bulwa and Chittagong.

BAMFORD, a hamlet in the p. of Hathersage, co. of Derby, 4 m. N by W of Castleton, on a headbranch of the Derwent. Pop. in 1841, 297.—Also a township in the p. of Middleton, Lancashire, 3 m. W of Rochdale. Pop. with Birtle, 1,753.

BAMIAN, or BAMEEAN, an ancient town of Afghanistan, on the l. bank of the river of the same name, to the S of the Akrobat pass, 55 m. WNW of Cabul, in N lat. 34° 50', E long. 67° 48', at an alt. of 8,496 ft. above sea-level. This celebrated city, called the Thebes of the East, rises on the acclivities of an insulated mountain in a valley of the same name, and exhibits amidst its ruins numerous excavations in the rocky steeps. Of these—the temples and dwelling-places of its former inhabitants-12,000 were reckoned by Abul Fazel to exist in the district. They are scattered over both sides of the valley throughout an extent of 8 m.; and still form the residence of a great part of the pop. The hills here being formed of indurated clay and pebbles, are easily excavated; but the great extent of these excavations excites surprise. Some of them have no pretensions to architectural ornament, being mere square holes; but others are finished in the shape of a dome, and have a carved frieze below the point from which the cupola springs. But the most remarkable monuments of antiquity which they present, are two colossal figures, 50 cubits in height, cut in bold relief, erect in niches in the mountain's side. At a short distance is another of much smaller dimensions. By some they are supposed to be the productions of the 3d cent., during the dynasty of the Sassanides; according to others, they are relics of Bhuddism. They are all much mutilated; and numerous fragments of similar sculptures lie scattered in the locality. The town was taken in 1220, and destroyed by Jenghiz-Khan. It was rebuilt, but was subsequently deserted by its inhabitants. Several passes lead from the valley of B. across the Hindukush on the E, and the Paropamisan mountains on the W. BAMIDU'NIAH [i. e. 'the Roof of the world'], a name given by the natives to that portion of the great Asiatic table-land of Pamir, where the Oxus has its fountain-head in the Sir-i-kol, a lake 14 m. long from E to W, by about 1 m. in average breadth, the W end of which Wood places in N lat. 37° 27', E long. 73° 40', and the alt. of which above sea-level he calculates at 15,600 ft.—Journey to the Oxus.
BAMINICHE, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of and near the l. bank of the Sonora, 30 m. SSW of Arispe.

BAMINWAS, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 58 m. from Jypur. It is situated on a steep and rocky eminence, which rises abruptly from the surrounding plain. It contains some Hindu temples; but the houses are generally poor and ill-built.

BAMMAKU, a town of Western Africa, in the kingdom of Bambarra, on the l. bank of the Niger, 135 m. SW of Sego. It possesses considerable commerce, especially in salt. The navigation of the river above this point is much impeded by rocks.

BAMORI, or BAMOUREE, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, on the northern confines of the Bareilly district, 25 m. SSW of Almora, with which it is connected by a magnificent road, carried at an alt. of 7,200 ft. over the summit of the Goggar mountain. B. is situated at the base of the Kumaon range, on the r. bank of the Kosee, in the great Jaul forest. In the vicinity is a commissariat depot. ~ BAN

BAMPTON, a hundred or division of Oxfordshire, bounded by Chadlington hundred on the N; Wooton hundred on the E; Berkshire on the S; and Gloucestershire on the W. Area 42,070 acres. Pop. in 1841, 15,628.—Also a hundred in Collumpton division, Devonshire. Area 29,430 acres. Pop. 6,990.

BAMPTON, or BATHAMPTON, a market-town and p. in the co. of Devon, 161 m. W by S of London, and 6 m. N of Tiverton. It contains the hamlets of Petton and Shillingford. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the manufacture of serge. The town was formerly a borough, and sent two members to parliament. It now votes in the election of a countymember for N Devon. Pop. in 1801, 1,364; in 1841, 2,049. Area 8,130 acres.

BAMPTON WITH WEALD, a parish in Oxfordshire, 6 m. NE of Farringdon in Berkshire. It comprises the market-town of Bampton, the chapelry of Shifford, and the hamlets of Aston with Cote, Chimney, and part of Bright-Hampton. Pop. with Weald, in 1801, 1,003; in 1841, 2,734. Area 10,250 acres.
BAMPTON, a parish and township in Westmoreland, 4 m. NW of Shap. It lies along the river Lowther, and stretches SE to Haweswater. Pop. in 1801, 600; in 1841, 579. Area 10,390 acres.
BAMPTON (KIRK), a parish in Cumberland, 63 m. W of Carlisle. It comprises the townships of Kirk-Bampton, Little Bampton, and Oughterby. Pop. of the p. 536; of the t. 193. Area of p. 3,580 acres.
BAMPTON (LITTLE), a township in the above p., 5 m. N by E of Wigton. Pop. 212.

BAMPTON SHOAL AND ISLANDS, a crescentshaped reef in the Coral sea, between the NE coast of Australia and New Caledonia, extending between 18° 45' and 19° 30' S lat., and between 158° 10' and 158° 50' E long. It is chiefly coralline, but encloses a depth on a bed of fine shelly sand of 45 fathoms. The two islands on the N and E of the reef are covered with trees.
BAMUA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, on the r. bank of the Sinaloa, 12 m. above its embouchure in the gulf of California, and 45 m. SW of the town of Sinaloa.
BAMUCHARS, a district of S Africa, in the territory of the Bichuanas, watered by the Kuruman or Kruman river. Its chief town is Patana.

BAN, or Banal-Militar-Granze, an administrative subdivision of the Austrian empire, on the military frontiers of Croatia. It is divided into the Erster-Banal-regiment and the Zweiter-Banal-regiment. The former, occupied, as its name imports, by military colonists, has an area of 25.3 German, sq. m., with a pop. in 1837 of 56,193; and the latter, including the l'etrinia community, and the Rostainicza community, an area of 24.7 German sq. m., with a pop. in 1837 of 59,767. The arable surface of the whole amounted to 176,056 jochs of 1.4 English acres each; the forests to 127,190 jochs.

BAN, or Banovics, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 15 m. SE of Trentschin, on a hill near one of the head-streams of the Neutra. It has an active commerce in wool, iron, and cattle, and has 9 annual fairs.
BAN-SUR-MEURTHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, and cant. of Fraize. Pop. 1,765.
BAN-DE-LA ROCHE. See WALDRACH-INSTEINTHAL.
BAN-DE-SAPT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Senones. Pop. 1,482.
BANABINHU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, rising in the Sierra de Boa-Vista, and flowing N to its confluence with the Jaguaribe, 3 m. below which the united streams join the Salgado.
BANAGANPILLY, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, in the ceded districts of Ballagaut, 40 m. ENE of Gooty, near the base of a low range of hills, which contains diamond-mines.

BANAGHER, a market and post-town, and formerly a parl. borough, in the p. of Reynagh, King's co., on the l. bank of the Shannon, 2 m. SSW of Shannon harbour, and 64 m. WSW of Dublin. The principal street is rather spacious, and not very irregular. The communication at B. between Central and Western Ireland, was anciently inferior in importance only to that at Athlone, and was guarded with similar vigilance, and placed under the surveillance of similar fortifications. The bridge, built in 1759, was 400 ft. long, and had 18 arches. A canal, for conveying vessels past a local obstacle in the river, commences immediately above the old bridge, on the Connaught side, and is spanned by an arch on a line continuous with that of the old bridge. A new bridge was finished in 1843, immediately below the old bridge. It consists of 6 elliptical arches of 60 ft. span, and a navigable opening of 40 ft.; with an iron swivel-bridge, 45 ft. span. The length from abutment to abutment, 480 ft.; water-way, 400 ft.; length of bridge and approaches, 1,380 ft. The old bridge has been removed; and the shoals in the river are now in process of removal; when this is effected the navigation will be open from Killaloe to the entrance of the Grand canal at Shannon harbour, a distance of 38 m. B. enjoys important and far-ramified facilities for trade: it is an advantageous commercial depot for a considerable extent of country on both sides of the Shannon; it commands the whole of that great river's navigation, downward to the sea, and upward to Lough Allen; and it stands at only 2 m. distance from the point where the Grand canal crosses the river, and affords an outlet to Ballinasloe on the W, and to Dublin and all intermediate places on the E. It, in consequence, conducts a considerable trade in the export of corn and other provisions; and is also celebrated for its horse and cattle fairs. Pop. in 1831, 2,636; in 1841, 2,827. Houses 445.— Also a parish in co. Londonderry. Area 32,475 acres. Pop. 5,810. Mount Sawel, on the southern boundary, has an alt. of 2,236 ft.
BANAI, a town of W Africa, in the territory of Footatoro, 30 m. SE of Sedo.

BANAL, or BANAUL, an elevated valley of Hindostan, in the prov. of Cashmere, 10 m. in length, extending into the inosculation of the Himalaya mountains, which forms the S boundary of the prov. It is watered by the Jelum, and is fertile and salubrious, but it is allowed to remain uncultivated, in order that it may afford no facilities of invasion to the neighbouring states. The village, of the same name, is 48 m. SE of the town of Cashmere.

BANALBUFAR, a town in the island of Majorca, 11 m. NW of Palma, on the summit of a steep and richly cultivated hill, the terraced acclivities of which are covered with vines and olives, and which contains marble quarries. Pop. 5,000.

BANAN, a district of Africa, on the r. bank of the Joliba, and extending far to the E. Its inhabitants are of the Mandingo race, and are Mahommedans. They possess numerous herds of oxen, sheep, and goats, and manufacture cotton-stuffs and woollen cloth. They make voyages to Jennéand to Timbuctu. - Caillie.

BANA'NA ISLANDS, a group of three islands on the W coast of Africa, at the SW extremity of the peninsula of Sierra Leone. They are all inhabited. The largest, named Banana, situated in N lat. 8° 8', is about 4 m. long, and 4 m. broad, and is elevated and very fertile. The tides rise here at the equinox 9 or 10 ft. They are rendered famous by their connexion with the early history of the Rev. John Newton. BAN

BANANAL, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Santo Paulo, on the road between Rio Janeiro and Santo Paulo.—Also a village in the prov. of Rio Janeiro, in the district of Rezende.-Also a large island in the prov. of Goyaz, formed by two branches of the Araguay. Its S extremity lies in 12° 30' S lat. It is covered with a vast forest, and has a large lake in the centre. The soil when cleared is extremely fertile.—Also a small river in the prov. of Santo Paulo, which flows into the Parahiba on the r. bank.
BANANAS, a river of Guatemala, in the prov. of Costa Rica, which falls into the Caribbean sea, in N lat. 9° 55'.
BANANEIRA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, and district of Cabo-Frio, which joins the Santo Joano, on the l. bank. - Also the third fall of the Guapore, in S lat. 12° 35'.
BANARA, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Laristan, 50 m. NW of Lar.
BANAS, a river of Asiatic Turkey, rising some hours to the NE of Bunarbashi, and, according to Arundell, flowing S to the Meander.
BANASS. See BUNAS.
BANASSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lozere, cant. of Canourgue. Pop. 1,818.
BANAT, or Banal, a name employed in Austrian topography to designate certain districts in Hungary, Slavonia, and Croatia, which are placed under the superintendence of a ban or military regent.

BANAT OF TEMESVAR, an ancient prov. of Hungary, of which Temesvar was the cap., and which is now divided into the comitats of Torontal, Temesh, and Krassova, forming what is called the Hungarian Banat, which, with the German Banat, forms the Military frontier of Hungary on the side of Turkey. The emperor Ferdinand first quartered German troops in these districts of Croatia bordering on Styria, in order to protect Hungary against the incursions of the Turks. This garrison received numerous reinforcements of Servian and Croatian fugitives from Turkey, who received gifts of land on condition of acting as frontier-guards against the Turks. Every male is by birth a soldier, obliged to serve a certain time to learn his duties, and ever afterwards to hold himself ready at any moment to leave the plough or the loom for the sword and musket. Thus a formidable militia is maintained at little expense. These regiments are called Grenzer, from grenze, 'a frontier.' [Elliot.] In this way was first organized the military frontier of Styria, or the Croatian Banal or Banat already noticed. As the conquests of Austria extended in Hungary, this frontier line of military colonists was lengthened, until it finally extended round Transylvania in 1765-6, and was peopled by Usbecks, Croatians, Albanians, Macedonians, Servians, and Wallaachians. To these have been added colonies of Italians, Spaniards, Frenchmen, Germans, Dalmatians, and Bulgarians. The principal nobility of the three comitats of the Hungarian Banat are of Servian or of Armenian origin. The area of this Banat, which stretches from the Karasch to the frontiers of the Siebenburgen, is about 103 German sq. m.; and the pop. may amount to 82,000. The Teutsch-Banat, or German Banat, lies to the SW of the former, between the Theiss and the Karasch. Its area is about 70 German sq. m. Pop. 94,000.

BANAT-GRANTZE, or BANAT-GENERALAT, a subdivision of Croatia, organized in 1696. It consists of two circles, known as the Deutsch and the Wallachisch regiments; and comprises an area of 174 Austrian sq. m., with 2 towns, and 179 villages; and a pop. in 1837 of 119,397.
BANAWARAM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 80 m. NW of Seringapatam. It is situated in a plain, and consists of about 500 houses and a mud fort.
BANBECULA. See BENBECULA.

BANBRIDGE, a market and post-town in the p. of Seapatrick, co. Down, on the l. bank of the Bann, 2 m. NE by N of Loughbrickland. Within the last few years B. has risen to considerable importance, and become the acknowledged metropolis of a comparatively extensive linen-manufacturing district. Pop. in 1831, 2,469; in 1841, 3,324. Houses 573.

BANBURY, a hundred in the northern part of the county of Oxford; bounded on the N and E by Northamptonshire; on the S and W by the hundred of Bloxham and by Warwickshire. Pop. in 1831, 11,449; in 1841, 12,314. Area 21,230 acres.

BANBURY, a borough, market-town, and parish, in the above hund., 69 m. NW of London; 12 m. NE of Chipping-Norton; on the great post-road from Aylesbury to Southam. The p. comprises the borough of B., and the hamlet of Neithrop. The town stands on the small river Charwell. One member is now returned to parliament by the parish of Banbury. The number of electors in 1837, was 371; in 1842-3, 393. The manufacture of horse-girths and plush, formerly a principal branch of employment here, has declined; but its fairs and large weekly markets attract a considerable amount of trade. B. formerly gave the title of Earl to the Knollys family, a claim to the succession of which has recently been advanced, but without success .- Pop. of the town, in 1841, 3,787; of the p., in 1831, 5,906. Pop. of the parl. borough, which is coterminous with the p., in 1841, 7,366. Near the town are several mineral wells; and the pyrites aureus, or golden-firestone, is frequently found here in digging wells.

BANCA, or BANKA, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, separated by the straits of the same name from the NE coast of Sumatra. It lies between 1° 30' and 3° 8' N lat.; and between 105° 12' and 106° 55' E long.; presenting an extreme length of 140 m. from NW to SE, and an average breadth of 35 m. Its direction follows that of Sumatra and the Malayan peninsula; and its elevated parts have the same constitution as the great continental chain of Asia, being composed principally of granite, after which occurs a species of red iron-stone, extensively distributed in situations of secondary elevation. [Horsefield.] Groups of lofty hills are scattered over the surface. The Gunung-Maras, an isolated mountain which rises to the height of 3,000 ft. above sea-level, and the Gunung-Manum-Bling, or Manopin, in the western extremity of the island, form important landmarks in the navigation of the adjacent seas.— The principal rivers are the Jebus or Debus, and the Pyring, on the W coast; the Marawang on the E; and the Autun and Layang, which flow into Klabat or Tasse bay on the N. All the embouchures of these streams are barred with sand-banks, and the coasts are generally low. There are, however, several good harbours.—The soil is stony; but is generally well-watered and fertile. The forests abound with valuable species of wood, especially ebony; and abound in stags, wild boars, and a species of adder, the gall of which is said to be valued as a medicament by the Chinese. The climate is generally salubrious. The rainy season is from September to April; during the remainder of the year the SE monsoon blows. The principal production of the island is tin, which is found in all its alluvial districts, in deposits seldom exceeding 25 ft. in depth, lying between the veins of red iron-stone and the superincumbent granite elevations. The ore is the common oxide of tin; and in its original state is of a reddish brown colour. An amount of this metal estimated at 3,000,000 lbs. is annually exported, chiefly to the markets of China and Hindostan, ī. BAN where it is preferred to the tin of Cornwall. Some authors say that the B. mines were discovered only about the year 1710 or 1712; but it is known that the Arabs carried it to China in the 9th cent. [Asiat. Journ. vol. ix.] Honey, wax, and edible birds' nests also form important articles of commerce. The pop., amounting to 25,000, consists of Malays, Chinese, and Orang-Gunungs or aborigines. The latter inhabit the mountainous districts in the interior. They are of a pacific disposition; but are indolent and averse from the arts of civilized life. The Chinese, who are said to amount to 10,000, are chiefly miners. The Malays, who are the most numerous, inhabit the coasts, living in small prows, and subsisting by fishing.-In 1813 B. was ceded to the British by the sultan of Palembang, he however retaining its nominal sovereignty. In 1817 its possession was transferred to the Dutch. The principal town is Mantok, which is a Dutch settlement.—Stavorinus.—Staunton.—Marchand.

BANCA, or BANKA, a small island in the Asiatic archipelago, near the NE extremity of the island of Celebes, from which it is separated by a narrow strait. It has a harbour on its southern coast. It produces cocoa nuts, and limes, and its shores abound with turtle and other varieties of fish.

BANCA, or BANKA (STRAITS OF), the channel which separates the island of the same name from Sumatra. It runs ESE and SSE, and is about 34 leagues in length, with a breadth varying from 4 to 7 leagues. It presents formidable difficulties to navigators from the number of shoals and reefs with which it is obstructed, and its inequality of depth, at some points not exceeding 4 fath. The tide rises and falls about 11 ft. At the southern extremity is the small islet of Lucepara. BANCALAN, BANCALLAN, or BANKALAN, a town and fort of the island of Madura, in the Asiatic archipelago, situated in a pleasant, well-cultivated, and populous district, on the straits of Madura, in S lat. 7° 2′, and E long. 112° 45′. It is the capital of a small state of the same name.
BANCALAN, a small island of the Philippine group, near the S extremity of Palawan, in N lat. 8° 14'.
BANCALIS, or BANKALIS, an island in the straits of Malacca, separated by a narrow channel from the coast, and forming a section of Sumatra.

BANCAPUR, or BUNKAPUR, a district of Hindostan, formerly called Shahnur or Savanore, in the southern extremity of the prov. of Bejapur, and stretching along the northern bank of the Wurda and Tungabudra rivers. The chief towns are Savanore and Bunkapur about 6 m. apart, and the latter 43 m. SSE of Dharwar. It was formerly a place of considerable importance. Its fortifications were destroyed by Tippoo Saib.

BANCARO, a river of W Africa, which, after having watered the kingdom of Micoco, unites with the Congo or Zaire, of which it is supposed to be the principal branch, to the SE of Concobella. Its source is still unknown; but the general direction of its course is, on the authority of the natives, from the mountains of Nigritia.

BANCHORY-DAVINICK, a parish in the shires of Aberdeen and Kincardine, intersected by the Dee river, at the mouth of which it lies. The coast is bold and rocky, and has three small fishing-harbours, viz., Findon, Port Lethen, and Dounies. The Dee is crossed in this p. by a suspension bridge 305 ft. in length. Pop. in 1801, 1,557; in 1841, 2,736.

BANCHORY-TARNAN, a parish in Kincardineshire, bounded on the S by the Dee, which in this p. receives the Feugh. Pop. in 1801, 1,465; in 1841, 2,241, of whom 341 were in the v. of Arbeadie, and 66 in the v. of Bambury. 2 N
BANCO, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. 1 and 155 m. S of St. Marta, on the r. bank of the Magdalena, at the junction of the Sesar, and 30 m. SE of Mompos.
BANCOCK. See BANKOK.

BANCOORAH, a civil station of Bengal, 98 m. WNW of Calcutta, in N lat. 23° 20', E long. 87° 12', on the Great road to Benares. It is situated in a beautiful and salubrious district, watered by the Dalkissah, at an elevation of 215 ft. above sea-level, and 105 m. from the nearest part of sea-coast. The average temp. observed here in 1827 was 78.46 at 10 A. M.; and 79:56 at noon. The total fall of rain in 1827 was 54.228 inches; in 1828, 35.515 inches. The town is of recent origin, and is still of small extent.
BANCULIN, a group of rocky islets in the Philippine group, off the E coast of the island of Mindanao, in N lat. 8° 20', and E long. 126° 12'.

BAND, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, on a small bund or lake 15 m. ENE of Teary, and 60 m. SSE of Jaunsy.—Also a small town in the prov. of Gujerat, and dist. of Surat, the cap. of a pergunnah which is to a great extent covered with jungle. - Also a kingdom of Upper Guinea, tributary to Ashantee, and watered by the Coomba or Zimma. Its capital is 190 m. N of Coomassie.

BANDA', or BANDAH, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of the dist. of S Bundelcund, or the prov. and 95 m. W of Allahabad, on the r. bank of the Cane, in N lat. 25° 30'. It has rapidly risen to its present state of prosperity. The environs afford an excellent description of cotton, and the neighbouring hills consist of a fine grained granite.

BANDA ISLANDS, a group of small volcanic islands in the Eastern archipelago, about 130 m. ESE of Amboyna, and 45 m. S of Ceram; between 3° 50' and 4° 40' S lat. They are ten in number; Banda- Neira, Banda-Lantoir or Banda proper, Pulo Ay, Pulo Rondo or Run, Pulo Pisang, Rosingen, Craka, Capella, Swanji, and Gonong-Api. All are high, with precipitous mountains, and are of various dimensions, but none of them are large. Lantoir or Great Banda, averages 500 ft. in height, but is only 8 m. in length, by <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in breadth; and Neira, the next in importance, does not exceed 24 m. in length by 3 m. where broadest. Gonong-Api, to the N of Banda-Neira, rises in a volcanic summit to 1,940 ft. above sea-level; and its surface throughout is covered with sulphur and chalk.—Pulo Ay or Pulo Way lies 9 m. to the W of Gonong-Api; and Pulo Rondo, 4 m. further. Rosingen is 7 m. SE of Banda- Lantoir; and Pulo Pisang, 2 m. NE of Banda-Neira. One of the small islands is inhabited entirely by lepers. The chief produce of the Banda islands is nutmegs, for the cultivation of which Neira, Lantoir, Pulo Ay, and Pulo Rondo, were formerly laid out in plantations; but the cultivation is now confined to Great Banda. [Stokes.] This plant attains the size of a pear-tree, and has a leaf resembling that of the laurel; the fruit, enveloped in a membranaceous covering of mace, is contained in a husk. In nine months from the opening of the blossom, the fruit, which resembles an unripe peach, is gathered and put into a drying-house, where it is exposed during three months to a slow fire. The husk or shell is then broken, the mace, which partly envelopes the nut, is carefully separated, and the nutmeg placed among lime to prevent the attack of insects, to which it would otherwise be exposed. After it is removed from the lime, it is made up into packages of 200 lbs. each for exportation. Each tree produces about 10 lbs. weight yearly; and an oil is extracted from the unripe or damaged fruit. The nutmeg-tree requires constant care and shelter; a great proportion of them are barren, and this defect cannot be discovered be- BAN fore the 12th or 14th year, from which period they continue bearing until the age of 20, four years after which they perish. When the B. islands were captured in 1796, a half-year's crop was found to amoun: to 81,618 lbs. of nutmeg, and 23,885 lbs. of mace. The annual produce of Great Banda is now said to average from 300,000 to 400,000 lbs. of nutmegs, an 1 about one-fourth that quantity of mace. Crawford estimated the whole produce of the B. islands, in 1816, at 4,500 peculs or 600,000 lbs. of nutmegs, and 150,000 lbs. of mace. The inhabitants were formerly calculated at 15,000 whites. In 1796 they amounted to 5,763; and in 1814 they were reduced to about 4,000, composed of a few natives of Holland and half-castes, together with their descendants, and a number of convicts, and slaves. The European pop. in 1777 amounted only to 514; and in 1796 they were diminished to 119. The trade of these islands is held under the same impolitic monopolies as its agriculture. Formerly the Chinese, and other merchants established here, exported piece-goods, cutlery, and hardware, to Ceram, Papua, the Aru, and the SW isles, bringing in return slaves, sago, salted venison, pearls, tortoise-shells, and feathers. But the trade is now laid under restrictions for the purpose of preventing smuggling: and the Dutch governor taking advantage of this, has frequently brought supplies of merchandise from Batavia, and sold it to the oppressed inhabitants at a profit of 50 per cent. The government of the islands is established at Neira, which has a spacious harbour, and two fortresses, and is used as a penal settlement by the Dutch government. The garrison amounts to about 300 men, mostly natives of Celebes and Amboyna. The town is clean and neat; and contains, besides the government offices, ample storehouses for the reception of the nutmegs grown on Great Banda. A considerable number of Chinese are also settled here. On Lantoir, or Great Banda, there is also a fortress covering the harbour, but it is said to be a very unhealthy station. The water is bad; and the vapour which descends from the volcanic mountain of the neighbouring island, Gonong-Api, is peculiarly noxious. Under the Dutch there were seven islands, called the South-west islands, dependent on the government of Banda, on the principal of which, Kissier, there was a resident and a garrison of 50 men. The Portuguese first visited these islands in 1511, but maintained only a precarious footing on them. In 1608, the Dutch built a fort on Neira, and have, with the exception of the period from 1796 to 1801, and from 1811 to 1816, continued to maintain the monopoly of their commerce. In 1796 the British forces under Admiral Rainier took possession of the B. islands, without resistance, when they found 66,675 rix-dollars in the treasury, besides a large store of mace and nutmegs. They were restored at the peace of Amiens, in 1801; but on the renewal of the war, an expedition was fitted out against the eastern colonies of the Dutch, by which they were again captured in 1811. They were restored at the general peace of 1815 .- Stavorinus.—Bougainville.—Raynal.—Crawford.—Stokes.

BANDA ORIENTAL, a district of South America, on the great river Plata, between the parallels of 32° 10′ and 35° S, and bounded on the E by the Atlantic ocean, and on the W by the river Uruguay. The Spaniards gave it this appellation—signifying 'the Eastern frontier' or boundary-because to them it bounded on the N with Brazil or Portuguese America; while the Brazilians, on the other hand, termed it the Cisplatine province, on account of its position on the N side of the Plata. To both parties it formed a debateable territory, until, after a long and mutually exhausting struggle betwixt the Brazilians and the Buenos Ayreans, the dispute was terminated by treaty of the 27th August 1828, erecting the whole into an independent republic, with Monte Video as its capital. On the N, this territory is partially separated from the Brazilian prov. of Rio Grande by the Ybiqui Guazu flowing westwards into the Uruguay, and the Jaguarao flowing into the Atlantic. Its coast-line to the Rio de la Plata is about 240 m. in length. Along the Rio de la Plata it stretches 335 m. The Uruguay divides it into the Banda Oriental and Banda Occidental; but the latter district is nominally at least incorporated with the republic of Buenos Ayres, in which it forms the provinces of Entrerios and Corrientes. The republic, -which has become better known of late years under the title of the Republica Oriental del Uruguay, and will be described at length under that title,—is divided into the nine departments of Monte Video, Maldonado, Canelones, San José, Colonia, Soriano, Parsandu, Duragno, and Cerro-Largo. Its pop. was estimated, by Mr. Scarlett in 1834, at only 100,000. In the Almanach de Gotha for 1849 it is estimated at 250,000. This pop. is chiefly a mixed race of Indian and Spanish descent. The principal towns are the ports of Monte Video, Maldonado, and La Colonia or Colonia del Sacramento. Besides these there are 15 smaller towns. The interior of the country consists of one vast expanse of open undulating plains, intersected by small streams, whose margins, overrun with thickets, can only be penetrated at certain passes opened for the convenience of travellers. Ostriches, deer, and wild horses, abound in these plains; and the fastnesses on the banks of the rivers are infested by the ounce and the jaguar.—A desolating war has been carried on for several years betwixt Buenos Ayres, or rather its Guacho dictator, General Rosas, and the Monte Videans: the former supporting the pretensions of Manuel Oribe to the presidency of the Uruguay republic; while the Monte Videans at least support the rival candidate Fructuoso Ribera. It would appear that France, during her blockade of Buenos Ayres, stirred up the Monte Videans to declare war against General Rosas, in 1838, but having patched up a separate peace for herself in 1840, she left the Oriental state and its governor, Ribera, to shift for themselves. Rosas, who was not likely to forget or forgive the part which Ribera had taken with the French, took the earliest opportunity to concentrate his forces against the republic of the Uruguay; and in 1842 he had completed all his plans for the invasion of that country. About the middle of that year, the British and French ministers, in the name of their respective governments, offered their joint mediation to put an end to the war, which was at once declined by Rosas. In the course of the same year, however, a treaty of commerce and friendship was concluded between the republic of the Uruguay and Great Britain. Having refused the offered mediation, Rosas proceeded in his plans; and on the 6th of December, 1842, his forces, under the command of General Oribe, whom he supported as the legitimate president of the Oriental republic, encountered an army commanded by Ribera at the Arroyo Grande, in the Argentine province of Entrerios, and so completely routed it that the war was considered to be at an end, for there was no force of any description left to oppose the Buenos Ayrean army from marching to Monte Video, and placing Oribe at the head of the Uruguay government. But on the 16th of December, the British minister officially intimated to the governments of Buenos Ayres and Monte Video, that Great Britain and France had determined to put an end to the war, and demanded that neither army should enter the territories of the other. Rosas treated this intimation (unsupported as it was by a sufficient force) with contempt, and I half-a-mile below Innishannon. BAN immediately pushed his army across the Uruguay, while Mr. Mandeville, following up his intimation or the 16th of December, wrote to the minister for foreign affairs in Monte Video, urging him in the most earnest terms to defend the town, and assuring him of speedy assistance. This led to an immediate alteration in the state of affairs in the capital of the Uruguay republic: all parties, including many foreigners, united in preparing for the defence; the town was intrenched, every man capable of bearing arms was enrolled, and when Oribe, at the head of his victorious army, which had marched through the whole country unopposed, arrived off Monte Video, he found it in so effectual a state of defence that he could make no impression upon it. The united action of the French and English governments has since these events opened up the navigation of the Plata; but the struggle betwixt the Buenos Ayreans and Monte Videans has not yet yielded to the efforts of diplomacy.
BANDE, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia, 2 m. S of Orense, near the r. bank of the Lima. It is the capital of a judicial partido, and possesses thermal springs and baths. Pop. 2,400.
BANDEIA, a volcanic mountain of Senegambia, between the Senegal and the Gambia, in the Futa Jallon territory.
BANDEL, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, on the gulf of Tutomi, in N lat. 34° 45'. It possesses considerable commerce. Fishing forms one of the principal occupations of the inhabitants.
BANDEL D'AGOA, or NEGRO BAY, a seaport of E Africa, on the coast of Zanguebar, 300 m. SSW of Cape Guardafui. It is supposed to be the Zergifa of Ptolemy.
BANDELL, an old town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, in the district and immediate vicinity of Hoogly. It is of Portuguese origin; and contains a Roman Catholic church and a monastery.
BANDERMALANKA, or BUNDERMALANCA, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the N Circars, on an arm of the Godaveri of the same name, 3 m. above its confluence with the sea, and 45 m. ENE of Masulipatam.
BANDERMASSEN. See BANJARMASSIN.
BANDERO, a rocky islet in the Grecian archipelago, between Paros and Antiparos.

BANDIAT, a river of France, which takes its rise in Mount Brandeix, near Chalus, in Haute Vienne; runs in a SW direction to Nontron, in the dep. of Dordogne; thence bends WNW, passes Marthon in the dep. of Charente; and, uniting with the Tardoire, flows into the Charente near Mansle.
BANDITTI ISLAND, an island in the Asiatic archipelago, at the southern entrance of the straits of Lombok, in S lat. 8° 40', E long. 115° 25'. It is about 20 m. in circumf.

BANDOL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Ollioules. It is situated on the island of Pomègue, 10 m. from Toulon; and possesses a good and capacious port, the shipping of which in 1837 amounted to 14,556 tons. It has also a numerous fleet of fishing-boats. The locality affords a good species of red wine. Pop. 1,575.

BANDON, a river of co. Cork. It rises at a point between Owen mountain and Mullanamosty, only 55 m. E by N of the head of Bantry-bay. Its course to Innishannon, a distance of about 24 m., is prevailingly to the E; thence it is SSE; and thence, till its expansion into Kinsale harbour, and through that estuary to the sea, it is to the E and SE. It passes Dunmanway about 43 m. from its source. The town of B., 201 m. below its source, and Kinsale on the l. shore of its estuary, are the principal places on its banks. The river is navigable to Colliers' quay,

BANDON, or BANDON-BRIDGE, a market-town and a borough, partly in the p. of Ballymodan, partly in the p. of Kilbrogan, co. Cork. It stands on both sides of the river Bandon, and on the road from Cork to Bantry, 10 m. NE by N of Clonakilty. Bandon is no longer what it originally was, a fortified town, or a huge garrison of colonists in the midst of a suspected and suspecting tribe of natives; but an ordinary seat of annuitants, tradesmen, and artisans, similar in all features to most other towns in the county, and differing from the majority only in being larger, better built, and more respectably inhabited. The woollen and cotton manufactures which at one time existed here have long been declining. The only improving departments of trade is the export of corn and other produce of the circumjacent country, and the usual retail trade common to large inland towns. The projected Berehaven line of railway approaches within 8 statute m. of the town. A railway has been more recently projected from Cork to Bandon, with an extension to Bantry. B. now returns one member to parliament. Constituency in 1841, 355. Pop. in 1831, 9,917; in 1841, 9,049.
BANDON, a town and port of Lower Siam, on a navigable river, about 2 m. above its embouchure in the gulf of Siam.

BANDONG, a town of the island of Java, on the W coast, in the vicinity of the volcanic mountain of Guntur or Gunong-Guntur, an eruption of which, in 1822, destroyed upwards of 80 kampongs or villages.
BANDOUR, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 6 m. W of Mons. Pop. 2,485.
BANDUGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the territory of Gundwana, and district of Boghela, on a rising ground 50 m. S of Rewah.
RANEAS. See Baniasa.
BANERAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NNW of Valencia. Pop. 2,500.

BANEZA (LA), a small town of Spain, in the prov. and 27 m. SW of Leon, at the junction of the Duerna and Tuerno, 1 m. above their confluence with the Orvigo, and 15 m. SE of Astorga. It is the cap. of a partido.

BANFANGH, a district of Assam, on the S side of the Brahmaputra, and immediately to the E of the great island of Majuli formed by inosculations of that river, about 30 m. in length, and 20 m. in breadth. Its principal towns are Dhapcotagown and Daliagown. BANFF—pronounced Bamff—a parish in Banffshire, bounded on the N by the Moray frith; on the E by the Deveron; and on the W by the Boyndie. The surface is pleasingly diversified, and is estimated in Robertson's map at 6,312 acres, and in the Old statistical account at 7,680 acres.—The burgh of Banff is situated in the NE corner of the p., on a peninsula formed by the influx of the Deveron into the Moray frith; in N lat. 57° 40′ 33″, W long. 2° 23′ [Fraser], 45 m. NW of Aberdeen; 80 m. E of Inverness; and 165 m. NE of Edinburgh. It unites with Elgin, Cullen, Inverury, Peterhead, Macduff, and Kintore, in returning a member to parliament. The parliamentary constituency in 1839 was 203; in 1842-3, 213. The parliamentary borough boundaries include the recently erected town of Macduff. The town of B. comprises several well-built streets. The church, and the town-house, are each handsome structures; and there are several very substantial private houses,—the sort for families of small private fortune. The town is usually described as consisting of two parts,—the upper and the lower town,-or the town, and the sea-town; between these, on an elevated piece of ground, stands the castle. The harbour, which lies to the N of the town, on the W side of the shallow bay, is neither commodious nor good, owing to the BAN continual shifting of the banks at the mouth of the river, and its exposure to NE gales; that of Macduff, at the opposite extremity of the bay, is much the better of the two. In 1818-19, about £18,000 were spent in improvements on the harbour, and a vessel drawing 12 ft. can enter the new basin at ordinary high water. In 1846, the arrivals in this harbour were 190 vessels of 11,560 tons, of which 16 vessels were from the colonies or foreign parts. The custom-dues amounted to £1,849; and the tonnage belonging to the port was 111 vessels of 8,714 tons. There are no manufactures in the town; but the fisheries are extensive, and there is a large annual export of fish from the port. The Deveron salmon-fishings are rented at about £1,800, and the fish caught at them are principally sent to the London market. Herrings are cured here, and exported to London, Ireland, and Germany. Live cattle, and grain, are also exported to London. The port of B. includes the creeks of Fraserborough, Gardenstown, Macduff, Portsoy, Port-Gordon, and Garmouth. The registered tonnage and shipping belonging to the port in 1834 was 67 vessels of an aggregate tonnage of 4,301 tons. The amount of customs' duty collected at the port in 1835 was £1,112; in 1837, £1,164.

BANFFSHIRE, one of the north-east counties of Scotland; bounded on the N by the Moray frith or the German ocean; on the E and S by Aberdeershire; and on the W by the shires of Inverness and Elgin. This county, according to Souter's 'Agricultural Survey,' published in 1812, is comprehended in an isosceles triangle on a base of 30 m. along the coast from Troup-head on the border of Aberdeenshire, to the influx of the Spey on the confines of Moray; its height being 64 m. inland from the shore. Measured on the latest and most accurate maps, the distance in a direct line between the two extreme points on the coast, is 34 m.; and from Troup, in a direct line running SW to Ben Macdhui, or to Cairngorm, both in the SW corner of the county, at the head of Glen-Aven, 67 m. At an average distance of 12 m. from the coast, however, it is contracted by Aberdeenshire on the E, and by part of Morayshire on the W, to a breadth of only 4 m.; so that, in its general form, it bears some resemblance to an hourglass. Making the proper deduction on this account, its surface is, according to Souter, 622 sq. m., or 315,600 acres Scots computation. By another admeasurement, its superficies is estimated at 647 sq. m., or 412,800 English acres. The course of the Deveron, in general, is accounted the boundary of Banffshire with Aberdeenshire; but the parish of Gamrie, and part of the parish of Inverkeithnie, are on the Aberdeenshire side of that river; while the greater part of the parishes of Cairney, Glass, and Cabrach, politically in the county of Aberdeen, are on the Banffshire side. Kirkmichael, the most upland district of the county, is bounded by the mountains which rise on the southern sides of Glenbucket and Strathdon. The river Spey may, with little impropriety, be deemed the general boundary on the W; although Morayshire extends in various places across that river likewise, into the parishes of Bellie, Keith, Boharm, and Inveraven. The principal rivers are the Deveron, the Spey, the Aven, and the Fiddich. The principal lochs are Loch Aven and Loch Builg. The great mountaintown being, to a considerable extent, a place of re- | knot in the SW corner of the county, at the point where the counties of Inverness, Banff, and Aberdeen meet, and composed of Cairngorm, Ben Buinac, Ben Macdhui, and Ben Aven, all surrounding Loch Aven, belongs to the Northern Grampians, and forms the highest land in Great Britain. Of these Ben Macdhui, on the S side of Loch Aven, in N lat. 57° 6', and W long. 3° 37', is in Aberdeenshire; and its alt., according to a recent admeasurement, is 4,390 ft., being 17 ft. higher than Ben Nevis. Cairngorm, which is common to Inverness-shire and Banffshire, has an elevation of 4,095 ft.; and Ben Aven, common to Aberdeenshire and Banffshire, has an elevation of 3,967 ft. Among the detached summits of the Grampians which entirely belong to Banffshire, are, Ben Rinnes, 15 m. SW by W of Keith, 2,747 ft. Corryhabbies, SE of Ben Rinnes, 2,558 — <math>2,500 -</math> Knock-hill, 12 m. SW of Banff, This county along the coast has, from remote antiquity, been divided into two districts. Between the towns of Banff and Cullen, the Boyne is the general name borne by the district; the tract between Cullen and the environs of Gordon castle is distinguished by the appellation of the Enzie. The whole of Banffshire, except the tract along the sea-shore, may be described as a hilly mountainous country, interspersed with fertile valleys welladapted to the cultivation of corn and grass. The hills, either covered with heath or moss, afford little pasture; while, from their bleak and barren aspect, they have a gloomy appearance. The arable landwhich bears but a small proportion to the wastelies on the sides and towards the bottoms of the higher hills; or on the sides of those valleys through which the streams have their courses. In several of these valleys, where cultivation has hitherto been found impracticable, there is abundance of fine healthy pasture, on which young cattle are raised to great advantage, the grounds being in general wellsheltered with natural woods. It is probable that at least 150,000 acres are now under cultivation, and that not above 80,000 are incapable of cultivation. In a general view the county of B. may be denominated a land of limestone, which, although it is not found in one continuous bed, over any extensive tract in the county, yet may be easily traced in almost every quarter of it. At Portsoy it passes into marble, or serpentine. In the Enzie district the calcareous matter, probably from a tinge of iron-ore, is in the form of stone marl of a dark red colour. In the upper extremity of the county, there are extensive beds of pure white marl; and of manganese, and iron-ore. Scotch topazes, or what are commonly called Cairngorm stones, are found in the mountains bordering with Aberdeen and Inverness-shires; and also in the forest of Mar. The principal productions are cattle, corn, and fish. The cattle are bought up by the dealers, from the first of May to the end of November, and sent off in droves to the southern districts. The corn and fish are exported by sea. There are in this county 10 fishing-towns, which employ from 100 to 120 boats. The fish which visit the shores are cod, ling, haddock, skate, whitings, holybut, dog-fish, and occasionally turbot and mackerel. The whole of the duke of Gordon's salmon-fishery, now let at the yearly rent of £8,000, may be stated as among the produce of this county. The salmon-fishing on the Deveron, of which the earl of Fife is the principal proprietor, is now let at an yearly rent of about £2,000 sterling.—The staple manufactures are those of linenyarn and linen-cloth, which at one time were carried on to a very considerable extent at Banff, Cullen, Keith, and Portsoy, and gave employment to a number of men and women in the different operations of sisting chiefly of Hindus, but including many Maheckling, spinning, weaving, and bleaching. There were likewise at Banff and Portsoy extensive manufactures of stocking-thread. There are several tanworks and some extensive distilleries in the county. The population, in 1755, was 37,574; in 1801, 35,807; in 1831, 48,604; and in 1841, 49,679. The number of families in 1831, was 10,855; of families engaged in agriculture, 4,264; of families engaged | CONTROL CONTROL DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CONTRE DE CON BAN in trade, manufactures, and handicraft, 2,456. The co. returns one member to parliament.—The parliamentary constituency, in 1839, was 717; in 1842-3, 774. Banff is the county-town. The other principal towns are Keith, Cullen, and Dufftown.-There are 19 parishes, and 10 parts of parishes, in Banffshire. The number of parochial schools, in 1834, was 25, attended by 1,774 pupils. The number of schools not parochial was 125, attended by 3,913 children.- A railway to unite Dufftown, Keith, Fochabers, and Port Gordon was projected in 1845.
BANFFY-HUNDAD. See HUNYAD.

BANG, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, on an affluent of the Nerbudda, 85 m. SW of Oougein. It has blast-furnaces and iron-forges; and in the environs are some curious remains of Buddhist temples excavated in the rocks.

BANGA, a town of India, in the prov. and 40 m. E of Silhet, at the junction of the Barak and Surmah, 105 m. ENE of their confluence with the Brahmaputra. It possesses considerable commerce. -Also a town of the island of Panay, one of the Philippine group, on the bay of Batan, on the N coast.

BANGALORE, a town and fortress of Hindostan, in the territory of the rajah of Mysore, situated in an undulating plateau, nearly 3,000 ft. above sealevel, 70 m. NE of Seringapatam, and 190 W of Madras; in N lat. 12° 57', and E long. 77° 38'. The pettah or modern town, which is of considerable extent, is surrounded by a double line of mud fortifications, interlined with a thick defence of jungle and bamboos. The streets are generally tortuous and narrow; and the houses, although in a large proportion of good dimensions, are entirely built of red earth, and roofed with tiles. The principal bazaar is uniform and spacious, and adorned with lateral rows of cocoa-nut trees; and there are several mosques and pagodas. In the outskirts of the town are magnificent gardens constructed by the last sultans, in which the cypress and vine grow in the greatest luxuriance, and the apple, peach, and strawberry are cultivated with success. The climate of the locality is remarkable for its salubrity and the equality of its temperature, its range never rising above 82° nor falling below 56°. The fortress, which is two miles distant from the modern town, is now in ruins, having been destroyed by Tippoo after its capture by the British. It is of great extent, and its construction of considerable strength. It is encompassed by a deep ditch cut in the solid rock and by a broad esplanade. Within its area was the original town or village of B., the walls of which are still to be traced, and the sultan's mahal or palace, a mud edifice now used as quarters by the officers of the British troops. The military cantonment is extensive and commodious, with gardens attached to the bungalows of the officers, a good race-ground, an assembly and a reading room, shops, &c. B. possesses in its central position great commercial advantages. Its trade, consisting in silk, cotton, dyestuffs, metals, spices, sugar, salt, and betel-nut, extends throughout all the south of India. Its principal manufactures are silk and cotton fabrics chiefly for local use, paper, and iron and copper utensils. The cotton-yarn is spun by the women, and sold at the weekly markets to the weavers. The pop., conhommedans, amounted in 1805 to 60,000. B. became incorporated with the Mysore in 1687. The present town was founded by Hyder Ali.
BANGANAPILLY. See BANAGANPILLY.
BANGAREH. See GANGES.
BANGASKA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Lower Wallachia, on an inosculation of the Danube, 30 m. S of Bucharest.
BANGASSI, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Manding, near the N bank of the Ba-Woolima, visited by Park in 1805.
BANGAY ISLANDS, a group in the Asiatic archipelago, at the southern entrance of the Molucca passage, and separated from the Tulla islands by Greyhound's strait.

BANGKHA, a thanah or division in the centre of the prov. of Bhagulpur in Hindostan. Area 997 sq. m. Pop. 226,000. It is watered by the Chandan, and the Khatuk rivers, and their tributaries.-The cap., of the same name, is a poor village of about 120 houses.
BANG-KHANG, a town of Siam, on the l. bank of a tributary of the Bang-pa-Kung, 180 m. NE of the embouchure of that river in the gulf of Siam.

BANGKOK, or BANKOK, the capital and principal trading-port of the kingdom of Siam, a large city situated on both banks of the river Menam, about 25 m. from its mouth, in N lat. 13° 40', E long. 101° 10'. The river is here about 300 yards wide, and from 5 to 10 fath. deep; and both banks, for a space of 3 or 4 m., are lined by 8 or 10 rows of wooden houses, floating on bamboo rafts, which are fastened at each end by long bamboos driven into the bottom of the river. These houses are constructed of boards only; and are of a neat oblong form, and built in Chinese fashion. The land portion of the city extends on both sides of the river to a distance of 200 or 300 yards from its banks, and, to protect them from the annual inundations, and even the daily tides, are built on posts driven into the earth: so that the river, and various canals branching from it, in fact form the streets or path-ways of the city; and are at all hours filled with light narrow boats or canoes passing swiftly in all directions. The king's palace is situated higher up the stream, on a narrow island from 2 m. to 3 m. in length, and surrounded by a brick wall. A considerable part of the space enclosed by the wall consists of waste ground, swamps, and gardens, and the greater part of the buildings are not much superior to the huts which form the balk of the city. The wats or temples are the only edifices of any pretension to magnificence. They are built in the Chinese style, and decorated with a variety of fantastic ornaments; and are always placed in favourable situations, and adorned with fine walks, pools, trees, and fragrant flowers and shrubberies. A gigantic seated figure of Buddha always occupies the central space of these temples, which are used as public schools, in which the children are educated by the priests without charge.—The pop. of B.—which is the only part of Siam with which Europeans are acquainted—was estimated in 1828, by a government census, at 401,300. This pop. appears to have rapidly increased since, as a recent number of the Singapore Free Press estimates it as follows:— English, Americans (missionaries and their wives), Siamese, Peguans, . . 10,000 15,000 Laocians, . . . . Burmese and Tavoyans, . 8,000 15,000 Malays, 636,821 The Chinese it thus appears form the most prominent part of the pop.; they are likewise the most efficient; so that B. to a stranger might seem a Chinese rather than a Siamese city. Slavery in a modified form exists to a great extent amongst this pop.; BAN and the missionaries describe the native Siamese of this city as excessively addicted to gambling and vice in all its forms, especially to the use of narcotics and intoxicating drinks.-The imports into the port of B. in 1846, amounted to about 1,130,000 ticals, or £120,000; and the exports to 1,500,000 ticals. This amount comprehends the trade with Singapore, Bombay, Batavia, Palembang, and England, but not the direct junk-trade with China, which is very large, but of which it is impossible to obtain an estimate. The trade between Siam and the neighbouring coasts is also of considerable importance. The king's commercial fleet is large, consisting of 10 square-rigged vessels, measuring altogether about 4,200 tons, and two or three vessels of medium size, at present on the stocks.—The principal imports are cotton piece goods, printed muslins, red cloth, cotton twists, silks, woollens, crockery, hardware, and Chinese 'notions' in abundance. The people already show a taste for European productions, and this branch of trade is on the increase. The articles most generally suited for imports are thus enumerated in the Singapore Free Press:- "White and grey long cloths; ditto madapollams; ditto cambrics; ditto jaconets; book lappets; velvets, plain and figured; checked fancy muslins; American drills; cotton umbrellas; figured long cloths, white and dyed; dyed cambries; dyed long cloths; prints, chintz, furnitures and neutrals; Siam chowls or dresses; Turkey red cloth; grey cotton twist; Turkey red ditto; imperial red and blue ditto; long ells; ladies' cloths; Spanish stripes; merinos of assorted colours; canvass; iron, steel, lead, and speltar; earthenware, assorted; glass ware and lamps, assorted; hardware, assorted; muskets, gunlocks; brimstone; beeswax; cowries; flint stones, musket flints," &c .- The principal exports are sugar, gamboge, sticklac, benjamin, cardamoms, raw silk, tin, horns, ivory, salt, fish, rice, sapan and rosewood, betel nut, &c. The sugar crop averages 160,000 piculs per annum. With proper encouragement and suitable machinery 2,000,000 piculs might be produced with as much readiness as the present amount. The sugar-season is from the beginning of January to the month of August. The Bangkok Calendar for 1848, published by the missionaries at B., supplies the following information respecting the trade of this place:—"The trade of Siam labours under numerous disadvantages. The duty on foreign square-rigged vessels is so high as to almost prevent them coming here, while the market is also at the same time very uncertain and fluctuating: Besides the annoyances from governmentofficers, the difficulties and detentions in procuring passes are so great that few except native vessels ever return a second time. Several vessels have been lately built by the Siamese after European models with junk-heads, which pay little or no duty, and therefore foreign vessels cannot compete with them. The duty on English and American ships or tonnage." - Crawfurd's Embassy to Siam. - Finlayson's Mission. - Asiatic Journal. - Singapore Free Press.—Missionary Register.
BANGLORE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, in the dist. and 22 m. SE of Bangalore, on the r. bank of the Penaukeny.

BANGOR, a city, diocese, and parish, in Carnarvonshire, 2361 m. NW by W of London, 9 m. NE of Carnarvon, and 4 m. E of Beaumaris. It stands at the foot of a steep rock, in a narrow and fertile vale, ----- 为其是在这种人的 PER MARINE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SER Street In the second second . . 4 . 2.7 A Pullerum & Co London & Edinburgh . near the N entrance of the Menai strait, and the mouth of the river Ogwen .- The dio. comprehends the island of Anglesea, the co. of Carnarvon with the exception of 4 parishes, 14 parishes in the co. of Denbigh, and 7 in that of Montgomery. The average gross yearly income of the see of B., in 1831, was £6,580: nett income, £4,464. The choir of the present cathedral was erected in 1496. Its dimensions are as follows: Length 214 ft.; length of the nave 141 ft.; of the cross aisles 96 ft.; of the side-aisles 60 ft.; height of the tower 60 ft.—The city of B. consists chiefly of one narrow street, nearly a mile in length. The principal buildings are the cathedral, the episcopal palace, the deanery house, the free school, the market-house, and assembly-rooms. From its proximity to the sea, B. has become a favourite bathing-place; and the number of visitors has been greatly increased since the construction of the Menai bridge. About 50 years ago there were only 93 houses in B.; since that period upwards of 800 have been built. The principal trade consists in the export of slates, which are raised in the quarries of Llandegai, 6 m. distant, and conveyed on a railway to Port Penrhyn. There is a good fishery near Garthferry, in the straits of Menai. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 1,770; in 1841, 7,232.
BANGOR, a parish in the co. of Cardigan. Pop. in 1841, 210.

BANGOR, a parish in co. Down, containing the town of Bangor, and the villages of Conlig, Crawfordburn, and Groomsport. Area 17,027 acres. Pop. in 1831, 9,355; in 1841, 10,060. The parochial limits include the Copeland islands. The whole p., excepting the islands, extends along the south shore of Belfast Lough. A lead mine was formerly worked in this p. by the Mining company of Ireland.—The sea-port, market, and incorporated town of Bangor, on the coast of the above p., stands around and near Bangor bay, a cove of Belfast Lough, 5 m. WNW of Donaghadee, and 10 m. ENE of Belfast. The cotton manufacture is carried on to a considerable extent in both the town and its vicinity; the linen manufacture also commands some attention; and much business is done in the export of provisions and live cattle. The port has 15 sail of carrying vessels, and a number of yawls. Bangor bay is 800 yds. broad and 600 long; its general depth of water is from 2; to 3 fath.; and its bottom is firm sand and good holding-ground. Pop. in 1831, 2,741; in 1841, 3,116.

BANGOR, a town of Maine, U.S., the cap. of Penobscot co., on the W side of Penobscot river, 60 m. from the ocean; in N lat. 44° 48', W long. 68° 47'; 68 m. ENE of Augusta, and 230 m. NE of Boston. Pop. in 1790, 169; in 1830, 2,868; in 1840, 8,627. It conducts an active commerce in lumber, employing upwards of 1,200 vessels, of above 100 tons each, in this trade. The harbour is formed in the mouth of the Kenduskeag, and the tide rises in it 17 ft.—Also a township in Franklin co., New York, 6 m. W of Malone. Pop. 1,289.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, and in the cant. and island of Belle-isle. Pop. 1,638.

BANGOR-MONACHORUM, or BANGOR-IS-YCOED, a parish, partly in the co. of Flint, and partly in the co. of Denbigh, 6 m. N of Ellesmere. It contains the townships of Bangor in Flint, and Eyton, Pickhill, Royton, and Sesswick in Denbigh. The v. of B. lies on the river Dee, in an open and fertile country. Richard of Circucester calls this place Banchorium Statio; and it is remarkable for a monastery which formerly stood here-of which, however, no traces now remain-said to have been one of the largest and most ancient in Britain. Pop. of the p. 1,257; of the v., 596. BAN

BANG-PA-KUNG, a river of Siam, which takes its rise in the mountains bounding Laos on the W; and after a course of 250 m. in a general direction from NE to SW, falls into the NE part of the gulf of Siam, at the town of Bang-pa-soi.
BANG-PA-MUNG, a town of Siam, on a small affluent of the Menam, 175 m. N of Yuthia.
BANG-PA-SOI, a town of Siam, at the confluence of the Bang-pa-kung river with the gulf of Siam, and 45 m. ESE of Bangkok.
BANG-PLAKOT, a town of Siam, on a tributary of the Bang-pa-kung river, 170 m. NE of Bangkok.
BANG-PO-MUNG, a town of Siam, on the l. side of the gulf of that name, and 30 m. S of Bang-pa-soi.

BANGSI, a division of the prov. of Goruckpur, in Hindostan. It is on the W side of the prov., and is watered by the Rapti and the Bangangga rivers. Area 687 sq. m. Pop. 18,165. The surface is in many places marshy, and much of it is flooded in the rainy season.—The cap. of the same name is a small town of about 600 houses, built on both sides of the Rapti.
BANGUELA. See BENGUELA.

BANGUEY, an island in the Asiatic archipelago, at the N extremity of Borneo, in N lat. 7° 25', and E long. 117° 10'. It is 23 m. in length, and 11 in average breadth; and is separated from Balambagar by a channel about 3 m. in width. Its coasts abound with turtle.
BANHALMA, a town of Hungary, on an inosculation of the Theiss and Brettye rivers, 48 m. WSW of Debrecin.
BANHAM, a parish in Norfolkshire, 2 m. NE of Kenninghall. Area 3,710 acres. Pop. 1,165.
BANHO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 14 m. NE of Viseu. Pop. 2,000.
BANHOS, a magnificent fall of the Ajuruoca river, in the Brazilian prov. of Minas-Geraes, in the district of Baependi.
BANI, a town on the S coast of Hayti, near the mouth of a small stream of the same name.
BANIACK. See BANJACK.
BANIANI, a town of Turkey, in Servia, on an affluent of the Save, 43 m. WSW of Belgrade.

BA'NIASA, a village of Syria, in the pash. and 50 m. N of Tripoli, at the confluence of a small stream with the Mediterranean, in N lat. 35° 12'.—Also a village in the pash. and 45 m. SW of Damascus, 23 m. E by N of Tyre, on a river of the same name, at the foot of Jebel Heish, the ancient Mount Hermon, which unites with the Jordan to the N of Lake Hoole. It is the ancient Paneas, and Casarea Philippi. The magnificent temple erected here by Herod, in honour of Augustus, has entirely disappeared. On the summit of an adjacent hill is a fort built in the 13th cent.
BANICA, a town of Hayti, in the dep. of Lozamo, and in a valley of the same name, on the l. bank of the Artibonite, 30 m. NE of Port au Prince.
BA-NIMMA, a considerable river of W Africa, in the kingdom of Bamburra, falling into Lake Dibbie, to the SW of Timbuctu, in N lat. 12° 40'.
BANISERAILE, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Dentila, in N lat. 13° 30'. It was visited by Park in 1805.
BANITRAL, a district and a pass of India, in the Punjab, on the S side of a mountain-range which bounds Kashmir on the S. The pass is difficult, and attains an alt. of 8,500 ft. above sea-level.

BANJACK, or Baniack Islands, a group in the Asiatic archipelago, off the W coast of Sumatra, between Hog and Nias islands. The principal, named Pulo Baniack or W Banjack, situated in N lat. 2º 10', E long. 96° 30′, is 18 m. in length, and about 7 m. in average breadth, and is distinguished by a lofty conical hill near its N extremity. It is inhabited by a tribe who use a peculiar dialect, and whose chief oc- i cupation is trepang fishing. BANJALUKA, BANGALUKA, OF BANIALAKA, a sanjak and town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Croatia, on the frontiers of Bosnia. The town is situated on both sides of the Verbas, 30 m. SSW of the confluence of that river with the Save, and 144 m. W of Belgrade. It is strongly fortified. It consists of 2,700 houses, generally ill-built, and contains two castles, numerous mosques, baths, &c. The suburbs are chiefly inhabited by Greeks. The commerce of B., promoted by the advantages of its situation, is active. Its chief manufacture is gunpowder. Pop. 15,000.

BANJARMASSIN, or BENJARMASING, [from Bendar, 'a port of trade,' and Masing, 'usual,' 'customary,'] a town and district of Borneo, situated on a considerable river of that name which falls into the sea near the S extremity of the island, and is navigable for at least 50 m. The district produces diamonds, gold-dust, iron, canes, and pepper, the last of | 110° to 115° W long. which is its staple commodity; it is also celebrated for the quality of its steel. There are imported here from Celebes, New Guinea, and different islands to the E, slaves, edible birds' nests, nutmegs, spices, wax, camphor, sago, antimony, and tortoise-shells, which are all re-exported from Borneo. The Dutch founded a commercial establishment on the banks of the river, at the end of the village of B. It consists of an octagonal fort, surrounded by palisades, with bastions towards the river side, and was built in 1709. An expensive establishment was also formed here by the English East India company in 1703, which soon afterwards was surprised, and partly destroyed by the natives. Successive but abortive attempts to establish commercial intercourse with this district were made by the English in 1736 and 1746. During the late war the Dutch fort was occupied by the British. The town formerly stood 18 m. up the river, but was transferred 6 m. lower down. It is in N lat. 3°, E long. 114° 55′; and consists of about 300 houses. A number of Chinese are among its inhabitants, who trade with various parts of the East, and with China. The imports are principally piece-goods, cutlery, gunpowder, fire-arms, and opium.—Stavorinus.—Raffles. -Crawfurd.-Keppel's Expedition to Borneo. Lond., 1846.—Earl's Eastern Seas.
BANJOUMAS, a town in the island of Java, on the S coast.
BANKA. See BANCA.
BANKALAN. See BANCALAN.
BANKAPUR. See BANCAPUR.
BANKARBAO, a town of Siam, on the western branch of the Menam river, 10 m. WSW of Bankok.
BANKASIR, or BAN-KISIR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Cutch, 27 m. NNE of Naggur Paakur or Parkhur, and 130 NE of Bhooj.

BANKEE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 30 m. W of Cuttack. It is the capital of a zemindarry of the same name, 30 m. in length from N to S, and 25 in average breadth, producing sugar, an inferior description of rice, cotton, oil, and tobacco.
BANKEL, or BANKELA, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, to the E of the island of Celebes, near the entrance of the bay of Tolo, in S lat. 2° 20', E long. 123°. It is about 21 m. in circumference.

BANKIBAZER, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, and 14 m. N of Calcutta, on the l. bank of the Hoogly. In 1724 it was ceded to the E India company, by whom it was fortified and rendered an important commercial emporium.
BANKERYD, a town of Sweden, in the district and 5 m. N of Jonkoping, on the SW bank of Lake Wettern.
BANK-NEWTON, a township in the p. of Gar- BAN grave, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 129.
BANKOK. See BANGKOK.
BANKS' BAY, an indentation on the W side of Albemarle island, one of the Galapagos group, in the S Pacific, in S lat. 0° 9', W long. 91° 30'.

BANKS' CAPE, a headland of Baranov island, in King George III.'s archipelago, off the SE coast of Russian America.—Also a headland of New South Wales, in Cumberland co., forming the N extremity of the entrance of Botany bay, in S lat. 34° 1', E long. 152°.
BANKS' ISLAND, an island in the N Pacific, one of the Princess Royal group, off the SE coast of Russian America, in N lat. 52° 47'. It is about 60 m. in length, and 5 m. in average breadth.
BANKS' LAND, or BANKS' PENINSULA, a district of Arctic America, separated by an arm of the Polar sea from Melville island. It was discovered by Parry in 1820, and has been coasted from about

BANKS' PENINSULA, an extensive projection on the E side of Tavai-Poenammoo or the Middle island of New Zealand. It is about 10 m. in length from NW to SE, and averages 5 m. in breadth; and is connected with the main by a low sandy neck of land. It consists almost entirely of steep rugged hills, and has a bluff rocky coast. Its northern shore is deeply indented by Pigeon bay, Port Ashley, and Toko-Labo harbour or Port Cooper. The two last have a common entrance 2 m. in width. About 1 m. to the W of the SE extremity of the peninsula, which is in S lat. 43° 52′ 15″, E long. 173°, is Akaroa harbour; and along the southern coast are several whaling-stations. Captain Cook thought this peninsula was an island; and as such it appears on his charts.

BANKS' ISLANDS, or SIR JOSEPH BANKS' ISLANDS, a group of four islands in the S Pacific, in the archipelago of the New Hebrides, between 13° 27' and 14° 11' S lat. The largest is about 16 m. in circuit.

BANKS' STRAIT, a channel in the S Pacific, which separates the Furneaux islands, lying to the S of Flinders, from the NE coast of Van Diemen's Land. It trends W by N 17 m.; is about 12 m. in breadth, with a depth of from 16 to 25 fath.; and is of easy navigation. All the trade of Hobarton, Launceston, and Port Phillip, passes through it.
BAN LUANG, a town of Siam, on the l. bank of the Menam, 15 m. N of Bangkok.

BANN, or Ban, a large river of Ireland. In consequence of its traversing Lough Neagh, or becoming lost for a great distance in that inland sea, it is usually regarded as consisting of the Upper and the Lower B. The Upper B. rises in that part of the Mourne mountains called the Deer's meadow, and at a point only 4½ m. W of the margin of the Irish sea. The main stream to the boundary between Down and Armagh, runs prevailingly to the NW. Soon after touching the co. of Armagh, it is joined from the S by the Newry canal, which brings up vessels from Carlingford bay; and thence it is navigable by boats of 50 tons burden NNW to Lough Neagh at Banfootferry, 2 m. E of the mouth of the Blackwater. Its length of course, measured in a straight line, is about 35 m. from its source to its embouchure.—The Lower Bann issues from Lough Neagh at a point 17 m. N of the embouchure of the Upper Bann; runs past Toome, where it is crossed by a ferry of grand communication between the counties of Antrim and Londonderry; expands, for 41 m., into Lough Beg; runs 14 m. NNW to the confluence of it with the Argivey; and then proceeds 10 m. NW to the Atlantic ocean, at a point 4 m. below Coleraine. Its entire course, from Lough Neagh downwards, measures, -exclusive of sinuosities, about 30 m. The river is tidal and navigable from the sea to the Salmon-leap at Coleraine; but the rapidity with which it meets the ocean, and the formidable bulk of the bar flung up by its conflict with the tide, render the navigation of its entrance so difficult, that only a skilful pilot can bring over a vessel of more than 200 tons burden. Projects for completing the navigation to Lough Neagh have long been entertained. The salmonfishery of the B. is celebrated for its productiveness.
BANN, or Banna, a small river of co. Wexford, It rises close to Ballykelly, and runs 18 m. SSW past Ferns to the river Slaney, at a point 4 m. above Enniscorthy.
BANNA, or Bana, a town on the frontiers of Persia, in Kurdistan, on one of the head-streams of the Tahite, 75 m. WNW of Senna.
BANNA, a large and navigable river of Lower Guinea, in Congo, which, running in a WSW direction, falls into the bay of Majumba in S lat. 3° 30'.
BANNA RAS, a promontory of E Africa, on the coast of Ajan, to the S of Cape Guardafui, in N lat. 10° 10′.

BANNALEC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, and arrond. of Quimperlé. The cant. comprises 4 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,940. The town lies 10 m. WNW of Quimperlé, on the road to Quimper. Pop. 4,183.
